coaching.coaching: Pin down compatible execution attestation predicate with a concrete worked example.
coaching.subject: compatible execution attestation predicate

coaching.coaching: Pin down prerequisite disposition vocabulary and attribution rules with a concrete worked example.
coaching.subject: prerequisite disposition vocabulary and attribution rules

coaching.coaching: Pin down review-phase enum schema and legacy sentinel with a concrete worked example.
coaching.subject: review-phase enum schema and legacy sentinel

coaching.coaching: Test the riskiest assumption behind 0.8 execution-phase priority floor constant first.
coaching.subject: 0.8 execution-phase priority floor constant

coaching.coaching: Plan how prerequisite-batch fidelity verification approach will be verified in-session — restate any deferred or unobservable success target as an observable proxy.
coaching.subject: prerequisite-batch fidelity verification approach

coaching.coaching: Record an ADR for inbound blocks scan indexing strategy — it reads like a one-way door.
coaching.subject: inbound blocks scan indexing strategy

coaching.coaching: Deliver plan scope beyond title-promised invalidation feature with existing machinery first; make the ideal version a dependent follow-on ticket.
coaching.subject: plan scope beyond title-promised invalidation feature

coaching.coaching: Run a quick pre-mortem on sibling file-overlap with no ordering edge: how could this plan fail?
coaching.subject: sibling file-overlap with no ordering edge

coaching.coaching: Pin down acceptance criteria completeness and measurability with a concrete worked example.
coaching.subject: acceptance criteria completeness and measurability

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the 'compatible execution attestation' predicate — what fields and values an execution attestation must carry to be accepted by execution mode — co-located with the phase acceptance rules.
evidence: Plan: 'Execution mode accepts planning attestations or compatible execution attestations.'
Plan: 'Execution attestations carry review-phase: execution and priority-floor: 0.80.'
No compatibility predicate definition appears anywhere in the plan — not in the close gate section, not in the enforcement section, not in the AC.
findings.finding: The 'compatible execution attestation' compatibility predicate is referenced but never defined. The plan states 'Execution mode accepts planning attestations or compatible execution attestations', but nowhere defines what makes an execution attestation 'compatible' — whether it requires matching priority-floor, a threshold comparison, a version check, or something else. This is a decision rule with no co-located schema.
findings.location: Settled design > Execution reviews and close gate
scenarios: An implementer must decide whether 'compatible' means (a) any execution attestation, (b) one with priority-floor >= 0.80, (c) one from the same ticket version, or (d) something else.
The close gate's phase check ('compatible phase') cannot be implemented without this rule.

evidence: src/rebar/llm/plan_review/attest.py: 5967-4476-7f9f-442d & 75de-02b0-d454-410b (no ordering edge)
findings.finding: Sibling tickets touch the same file(s) with no ordering edge.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a depends_on/blocks edge to serialize, or partition the file ownership.

evidence: 4 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the prerequisite consistency criterion's output schema: the disposition enum values, the per-prerequisite coverage record structure, and the attribution field name that Pass 2 verifies and Pass 3 uses for blocking decisions.
evidence: Plan: 'Every prerequisite receives an explicit disposition, and every blocking finding identifies exactly one canonical prerequisite ID. Unattributed findings are indeterminate and cannot block.'
Plan: 'Pass 2 receives the same subject/prerequisite pairing and verifies substance and attribution.'
No disposition enum or output schema for the prerequisite consistency criterion is defined in the plan or found in the codebase.
findings.finding: The prerequisite consistency criterion's output disposition vocabulary is never defined. The plan states 'every prerequisite receives an explicit disposition' and 'every blocking finding identifies exactly one canonical prerequisite ID', but the legal disposition values (e.g., consistent/inconsistent/inconclusive, pass/fail/warn) are not enumerated anywhere. Pass 2 must verify 'substance and attribution' against these dispositions, yet has no schema to verify against.
findings.location: Settled design > Prerequisite consistency review
scenarios: Pass 1 must emit a disposition for each prerequisite; without defined values, the LLM reviewer invents vocabulary inconsistent with what Pass 2 and Pass 3 expect.
The 'unattributed findings are indeterminate' rule requires a canonical attribution field; without a schema that field has no defined name or type.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the `review-phase` manifest line format (prefix string, allowed values enum, absence semantics) in the manifest schema section, co-located with the existing manifest field definitions in `manifest.py`.
evidence: Plan: 'Planning attestations carry review-phase: planning. Execution attestations carry review-phase: execution and priority-floor: 0.80. Phase-less legacy attestations count as planning.'
Plan: 'Planning mode accepts planning attestations only. Execution mode accepts planning attestations or compatible execution attestations.'
Confirmed via read of manifest.py build_manifest(): no `review-phase` line, no phase prefix constant, no parser exists — the plan introduces a new manifest field but never defines its serialization.
findings.finding: The `review-phase` field introduced by this plan has an undefined enum vocabulary in any schema. The plan uses the values 'planning', 'execution', and a 'phase-less' legacy sentinel in multiple decision rules (phase acceptance logic, close gate, pass-compatibility), but no formal enum or manifest line prefix/format is defined anywhere in the plan or in the existing manifest builder (`build_manifest` in `manifest.py`), making the serialization and parsing contract ambiguous for implementers.
findings.location: Settled design > Execution reviews and close gate; manifest.py build_manifest()
scenarios: An implementer must invent the manifest line syntax (e.g., `review-phase: planning` vs `phase: planning` vs a JSON blob), and if two implementers choose differently, attestations are unreadable across gate versions.
The close gate checks 'compatible phase' but has no schema to parse it against.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a `_REVIEW_PHASE_PREFIX = 'review-phase:'` constant and a `REVIEW_PHASE_VALUES = frozenset({'planning', 'execution'})` enum to `manifest.py`, document the absence→'planning' fallback rule there, and update `build_manifest` signature to accept an optional `review_phase` parameter.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Ground the 0.8 execution-phase priority floor in the existing threshold calibration data (`docs/experiments/plan-review-threshold-calibration.md`) or a new measurement, and document the derivation basis in the plan or a referenced ADR.
evidence: Plan: 'Execution attestations carry review-phase: execution and priority-floor: 0.80'
Plan: 'During execution, each blocking criterion uses max(its normal threshold, 0.8)'
ABSENCE: no reference to calibration data, measurement, or the existing threshold calibration experiment in `docs/experiments/plan-review-threshold-calibration.md`

Cached prior finding: `.rebar/cache/plan-review/jira-reb-1160/177bb253f03fbe1c8cb5.json` — '0.8 execution-phase scoring floor is asserted without any measurement, calibration study, or derivation basis'
findings.finding: The execution-phase review introduces `priority-floor: 0.80` as a hard design constant applied to all blocking criteria during execution mode. The plan states this value without any measurement, calibration study, derivation basis, or comparison to the existing threshold calibration work documented in `docs/experiments/plan-review-threshold-calibration.md`. A cached prior review already flagged this (`jira-reb-1160/177bb253f03fbe1c8cb5.json`). This is an unverified capability assertion: the 0.8 floor is asserted to produce a meaningfully stricter execution-phase review without evidence.
findings.location: Settled design / Execution reviews and close gate
scenarios: The 0.8 floor is higher than the calibrated block_threshold for some criteria, effectively overriding carefully-calibrated thresholds and introducing false positives that block valid execution-phase plans.
The 0.8 floor is lower than the effective threshold for high-priority criteria, providing no actual added stringency for the criteria that matter most during execution.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the unverified capability assertion about batch-packed prerequisite evaluations: either cite prior evidence/measurement that LLM batching preserves per-item recall and attribution at the required level, or specify that one-per-call is the default and batching is an opt-in optimization enabled only after fidelity testing confirms equivalence.
evidence: Plan: 'Multiple prerequisite evaluations may share a call when whole prerequisite blocks fit within existing context headroom. Batching is an optimization only: each evaluation asks whether the subject is consistent with one prerequisite.'
Plan: 'Packed behavior is fidelity-tested against one-prerequisite-per-call for recall, false acceptance, and attribution.'
ABSENCE: no citation of prior measurement showing batch-packed LLM evaluations maintain per-item recall and attribution quality vs. one-per-call
Context: ADR 0002 / ADR 0008 both cite specific measurement data (LLM call counts, wall-clock times) before committing to architectural choices; this sub-design does not.
findings.finding: The prerequisite consistency review batches multiple prerequisite evaluations into one LLM call ('Multiple prerequisite evaluations may share a call when whole prerequisite blocks fit within existing context headroom'), asserting that 'packed behavior is fidelity-tested against one-prerequisite-per-call for recall, false acceptance, and attribution.' This is a forward promise, not established evidence. The plan commits to the batching architecture before measurement, despite LLM multi-task batching being known to cause recall degradation and attribution confusion when tasks are co-mingled. No prior art or measurement is cited to justify that batching is safe as a default architecture rather than a post-measurement optimization.
findings.location: Settled design / Prerequisite consistency review
scenarios: The batching optimization ships as the default path; fidelity testing reveals recall degradation or attribution errors; the architecture must be unwound after code is written to the batching contract.

A prerequisite consistency finding in a packed call is assigned to the wrong prerequisite ID, triggering the 'unattributed findings are indeterminate and cannot block' rule, silently suppressing a real blocking issue.
findings.suggested_fix: Default the prerequisite consistency review to one-prerequisite-per-call (which the plan already defines as the fidelity baseline), and treat batching as an explicitly opt-in optimization gated behind the fidelity measurement AC rather than the default architecture.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a spike or calibration step to derive the 0.8 execution floor from historical review-score distributions (or existing test fixtures), and document the derivation basis in the plan before finalizing the constant.
evidence: 'priority-floor: 0.80' — stated as a constant with no derivation
'each blocking criterion uses max(its normal threshold, 0.8)' — the 0.80 value is asserted, not derived from historical review data or a calibration experiment
No spike, pilot, or measurement step for this threshold appears anywhere in the plan
findings.finding: The 0.8 execution-phase scoring floor is asserted without any measurement, calibration study, or derivation basis — making it an unvalidated threshold that will be difficult to justify or tune post-deployment.
findings.location: Settled design › Execution reviews and close gate — 'Execution attestations carry review-phase: execution and priority-floor: 0.80' and 'each blocking criterion uses max(its normal threshold, 0.8)'
scenarios: The 0.8 floor is too conservative for lower-risk ticket types, causing legitimate execution reviews to fail and forcing unnecessary re-reviews
The 0.8 floor is too permissive for high-risk changes, providing false confidence during execution
Post-launch, teams want to adjust the floor but discover it is not configurable and requires a code change

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Address how inbound 'blocks' sources are discovered at pin-collection time — either by specifying that the already-computed reduced ticket state (which exposes all blocking relations via the store's compiled state) is used, or by noting that a pre-indexed reverse-relation map is built once per review and shared across pin collection — so implementers have a clear performance contract.
evidence: 'Direct prerequisites are the union of outgoing depends_on targets and inbound blocks sources.'
Existing _find_direct_blockers() in src/rebar/graph/_blockers.py performs exactly this O(N) full-scan to find inbound blocks.
ABSENCE: no mention of how inbound blocks discovery is performed or its performance characteristics during pin collection.
findings.finding: Collecting inbound 'blocks' sources during pin collection requires a full O(N) store-wide scan (all tickets whose deps carry 'blocks → subject'), since that relationship is stored on the BLOCKER's ticket, not the subject's. The plan does not address this performance implication. At scale this scan runs at every plan-review call for every ticket that is a target of blocks relations.
findings.location: Settled design / Material and relationships
scenarios: A widely-depended-on ticket (e.g. a shared infrastructure epic with 50+ stories that block downstream work) triggers a full store-wide scan on every review-plan call for any of those downstream tickets.
A project with thousands of tickets performs this scan for each of them whenever plan reviews run in parallel during a sprint.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Introduce a configurable key (e.g. verify.execution_priority_floor) for the 0.8 execution floor, consistent with how the two enforcement flags are exposed, so teams can tune it without a code change.
evidence: 'priority-floor: 0.80' stored in attestation metadata as a fixed value
No corresponding verify.execution_priority_floor configuration key is mentioned alongside the two other configurable enforcement flags
Both verify.enforce_plan_material_pins and verify.require_plan_review_for_close are configurable, establishing a precedent the floor does not follow
findings.finding: The 0.8 execution-phase floor is hardcoded as a constant in the attestation schema and enforcement logic with no mechanism to configure or evolve it, despite being a threshold that is likely to require tuning as operational experience accumulates.
findings.location: Settled design › Execution reviews and close gate — 'Execution attestations carry review-phase: execution and priority-floor: 0.80'
scenarios: A project team discovers 0.8 is inappropriate for their ticket mix and must fork the review system or patch the constant
The rebar project enables enforcement but wants a stricter floor (e.g. 0.9) for a class of high-risk epics — currently impossible without code change
findings.suggested_fix: Add `verify.execution_priority_floor` (default: 0.80) alongside the other enforcement flags in the Settled design section, and reference it wherever the 0.80 constant appears.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either retitle the plan to reflect all three major feature clusters (pin invalidation, execution-phase reviews + close gate, prerequisite consistency review), or split the execution-phase and prerequisite-consistency work into separate sibling tickets with cross-references, so the stated title and the body's scope are aligned.
evidence: Title: 'Invalidate downstream plan reviews after child or prerequisite material changes'
AC3: 'Execution-phase reviews apply the 0.8 floor and the optional close gate requires a separate current review without treating implementation file changes as plan drift.'
AC4: 'Direct prerequisites receive a focused, token-budget-batched consistency review with per-ID coverage, verified attribution, and measured fidelity against one-per-call review.'
These two ACs describe features (execution phases, close gating, LLM consistency review) not entailed by 'invalidate downstream reviews.'
findings.finding: The plan does substantially MORE than its title promises: in addition to pin-based invalidation, it introduces execution-phase reviews (with a 0.8 priority floor), an optional close gate (verify.require_plan_review_for_close), and a full LLM-backed prerequisite consistency review criterion — all independent feature clusters not implied by 'Invalidate downstream plan reviews after child or prerequisite material changes.'
findings.location: Settled design sections: 'Execution reviews and close gate', 'Prerequisite consistency review'; Acceptance Criteria AC3, AC4
scenarios: An implementer reading only the title will not anticipate needing to build execution-phase tracking, close-gate enforcement, or batched LLM prerequisite review — leading to under-scoped effort estimates.
A reviewer approving this plan based on the title may not scrutinize the execution/close/prerequisite subsystems with appropriate depth.
Future epic decomposition may assign child tickets based on the title, missing these substantial sub-features.
findings.suggested_fix: Retitle to something like: 'Add upstream-change invalidation, execution-phase review gating, and prerequisite consistency review for plan attestations' — or decompose into three epics: (1) plan-material pins + invalidation, (2) execution phase + close gate, (3) prerequisite consistency review.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State concrete pass/fail thresholds for the batched-vs-one-per-call fidelity comparison in the Prerequisite consistency review section and AC4 (e.g., 'recall ≥ 95 %, false-acceptance rate ≤ the one-per-call baseline + 5 %').
evidence: 'Packed behavior is fidelity-tested against one-prerequisite-per-call for recall, false acceptance, and attribution.' — no numeric bound given
AC4: 'measured fidelity against one-per-call review' — 'measured' does not specify a pass threshold
No threshold appears anywhere else in the plan
findings.finding: The fidelity threshold for batched prerequisite-consistency review is never stated. The plan says 'fidelity-tested against one-prerequisite-per-call for recall, false acceptance, and attribution' and AC4 says 'measured fidelity,' but no pass/fail threshold (e.g., ≥95% recall, ≤5% false-acceptance rate) is given anywhere. An executing agent cannot write a passing test or know when this AC is satisfied without choosing an arbitrary threshold, which defers a real design decision to the executor.
findings.location: Settled design § Prerequisite consistency review ('Packed behavior is fidelity-tested…'); Acceptance Criteria AC4 ('measured fidelity against one-per-call review')
scenarios: Agent implements batching and writes fidelity tests but cannot determine a passing bar — either arbitrarily picks 80% (too loose?) or 99% (unachievable?), then ships either a false-passing or permanently-failing test.
Reviewer auditing AC4 cannot verify 'measured fidelity' is satisfied without an agreed threshold, blocking sign-off.
findings.suggested_fix: In the 'Prerequisite consistency review' section, add: 'Packed batching is considered equivalent when recall ≥ 95 % and false-acceptance rate does not exceed the one-per-call rate by more than 5 percentage points across the fidelity test corpus.' Mirror the same numbers in AC4.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the Outcome and Settled design propagation descriptions by making the Outcome explicitly say what propagates (material drift, not attestation invalidity) so both sections point implementers in the same direction.
evidence: Outcome: 'When enforcement is enabled, material drift in a pinned ticket invalidates the downstream attestation. Propagation is deliberately natural rather than eager: an upstream change invalidates its direct dependent'
Settled design: 'Attestation validity is not propagated. A related ticket's review can be invalid without affecting downstream tickets when its material did not change.'
The clarifying sentence in Settled design reconciles the two, but only if both sentences are read together; the Outcome's phrasing does not reference the distinction.
findings.finding: The Outcome section states 'an upstream change invalidates its direct dependent' (implying material drift propagates), while the Settled design's Material section states 'Attestation validity is not propagated' — an implementer reading both sections could take them as contradictory instructions about whether upstream changes cascade to downstream attestations.
findings.location: Outcome section ('Propagation is deliberately natural…') vs. Settled design → Material and relationships ('Attestation validity is not propagated.')
scenarios: An implementer reads only the Outcome and implements eager transitive invalidity whenever any ticket in the chain is re-reviewed.
An implementer reads only the Settled design and suppresses all downstream invalidity, breaking the direct-pin mechanism.
findings.suggested_fix: In the Outcome, change 'an upstream change invalidates its direct dependent' to 'an upstream *material* change (not mere attestation invalidity) invalidates the direct dependent's pin, so the downstream attestation becomes stale.' This mirrors the Settled design's clarifying sentence and removes the apparent contradiction.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a criterion (or extend AC3/AC6) that verifies the close gate correctly exempts bugs, session logs, code reviews, identities, and idea-to-closed transitions when require_plan_review_for_close is enabled.
evidence: Plan body: 'verify.require_plan_review_for_close defaults to false. When enabled, task, story, and epic close operations require a current certified plan review. Bugs, session logs, code reviews, identities, and idea-to-closed transitions remain exempt.'
AC3 only verifies 'the optional close gate requires a separate current review without treating implementation file changes as plan drift' — no mention of exemptions.
AC6 lists lifecycle test scenarios (toggling, legacy compatibility, natural transitivity, deletion, execution adaptation, concurrency, force-close) but omits exemption verification.
findings.finding: The close gate exemptions (bugs, session logs, code reviews, identities, idea-to-closed transitions) are fully specified in the plan body but no acceptance criterion verifies that these ticket types are correctly exempted from the plan-review requirement.
findings.location: Settled design → Execution reviews and close gate ('Bugs, session logs, code reviews, identities, and idea-to-closed transitions remain exempt.') vs. AC3 and AC6
scenarios: A reviewer passes AC3 with a test that only exercises task/story/epic close, never checking that a bug close is not blocked — the exemption silently breaks.
A regression removes the idea-to-closed exemption; no AC catches it.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC6's test scenario list to include: 'and close-gate exemption for bug, session-log, code-review, identity, and idea-to-closed ticket types.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend AC3 or AC6 to explicitly verify that execution mode persists across intermediate statuses and resets exactly when the ticket returns to open.
evidence: Plan body: 'Entering in_progress establishes execution mode. Execution mode persists across later statuses and resets only when the ticket returns to open.'
AC3 says 'Execution-phase reviews apply the 0.8 floor and the optional close gate requires a separate current review' — covers floor and gate but not mode lifecycle.
AC6 test scenarios: 'execution adaptation' is listed but is ambiguous about whether it covers mode reset.
findings.finding: Execution mode persistence and reset ('Execution mode persists across later statuses and resets only when the ticket returns to open') is a specified behavioral rule in the plan body but is not verified by any acceptance criterion.
findings.location: Settled design → Execution reviews and close gate ('Entering in_progress establishes execution mode… resets only when the ticket returns to open.') vs. AC3 and AC6
scenarios: An implementer resets execution mode on every status change rather than only on return-to-open; no AC catches it.
A scenario where a ticket moves in_progress → in_review → closed uses a planning attestation at close because mode was inadvertently cleared; no AC catches the regression.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC6 test scenarios: 'execution-mode persistence across in_review/done statuses and reset on return to open.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a documentation deliverable to the plan (e.g. an ADR or AGENTS.md section) capturing the key architectural decisions: pin-binding rationale, intentional exclusions from close-time validation, execution-phase design, and natural-vs-eager propagation choice.
evidence: 'Health and documentation' section describes only end-user-facing surfaces (CLI, MCP, audit, web UI), not architectural documentation for future maintainers
No ADR, AGENTS.md update, or design-doc reference appears anywhere in the plan

The pattern — embedding signed relational fingerprints into attestation metadata and enforcing them at multiple gate points — is novel and non-obvious; future contributors would need to rediscover the design rationale
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel cross-component attestation architecture (signed plan-material pins, execution-phase review, close gate) spanning review, enforcement, relationship resolution, CLI, MCP, audit, and web surfaces, but does not include an ADR, AGENTS.md section, or design-doc update to capture the architectural decisions.
findings.location: Settled design (all subsections); Health and documentation section — no mention of ADR or AGENTS.md
scenarios: A future contributor modifying the close gate does not understand why file-dependency hashes are intentionally excluded, introduces a regression, and the rationale is undiscoverable
A new engineer adding a relationship type (e.g. relates-to) does not know whether it should participate in pinning, because the decision criteria are undocumented outside the plan ticket itself

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add at least one AC with a concrete before/after validation: e.g., 'A lifecycle test demonstrates that a close attempt on a ticket whose prerequisite plan material has drifted is rejected (enforcement on) or surfaced as advisory (enforcement off), whereas the same close succeeded before pinning was introduced.'
evidence: AC1: 'New plan-review attestations deterministically contain complete, canonical plan-material pins' — system-internal state.
AC6: 'end-to-end lifecycle tests cover toggling, legacy compatibility, natural transitivity...' — test coverage, not user outcome.

ABSENCE: No AC describes a before/after comparison (e.g., 'a stale attestation that previously allowed close is now rejected') or an operational metric (e.g., 'zero stale-assumption incidents in rebar post-rollout').
Problem statement: 'an agent can therefore claim or close work using assumptions that no longer match' — but no AC verifiably demonstrates this is prevented.
findings.finding: No acceptance criterion carries a concrete before/after workflow comparison or operational metric validating that the user problem (agents acting on stale plan assumptions) is actually solved; all ACs describe system-internal state or test coverage rather than an observable improvement to the named user experience.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria (all six ACs)
scenarios: Reviewers cannot confirm the core user problem is solved because no AC creates a scenario where the old failure mode (agent closes with stale attestation) is demonstrated to be blocked.
Acceptance testing will pass all ACs while the actual user-facing benefit remains unverified.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC2 or AC6: 'A regression-style test documents the pre-fix behavior (stale attestation accepted at close) and confirms post-fix behavior (drift detected and gate triggered), exercised in the rebar project with enforcement enabled.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the authoritative source for execution mode: e.g. 'execution mode is derived by scanning the ticket's STATUS event history for an in_progress entry newer than the last open entry' (or alternatively, a new ticket field set on the in_progress transition and cleared on open), so the implementation has a single, unambiguous derivation rule.
evidence: 'Entering in_progress establishes execution mode. Execution mode persists across later statuses and resets only when the ticket returns to open.'
'Planning attestations carry review-phase: planning. Execution attestations carry review-phase: execution and priority-floor: 0.80.'
ABSENCE: no specification of where execution mode state is stored or how it is derived at review time.
findings.finding: The mechanism for 'execution mode' is ambiguous: the plan defines it as a ticket status-history concept ('enters in_progress → execution mode, resets on return to open') but attestations carry 'review-phase: execution' as metadata. The plan never specifies HOW execution mode is derived at review time — whether from a new ticket field, a computation over STATUS event history, or from the prior attestation's phase — leaving implementers free to choose inconsistent approaches (e.g. reading from attestation alone fails on first execution review; status-history requires scanning events).
findings.location: Settled design / Execution reviews and close gate
scenarios: Ticket transitions open → in_progress → blocked → in_progress: execution mode must persist across blocked. If derived from last STATUS event only, blocked→in_progress would appear as a fresh start and might incorrectly show as planning mode.

Ticket returns to open then reclaimed: execution mode must reset. If derived from attestation phase rather than status history, a stale execution attestation could incorrectly establish execution mode for the next claim cycle.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the 'Execution reviews and close gate' section: 'Execution mode is computed from the ticket's STATUS event history: a ticket is in execution mode when its most-recent open→in_progress transition is newer than its most-recent return to open (if any). This computation is performed at review time and at close-gate time from the already-reduced ticket state, requiring no new field.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief positive rationale (one paragraph in the Outcome or Settled design section) explaining WHY signed pins as separate attestation metadata with pull-based drift detection was chosen — e.g. consistency with the existing computed-on-read validity model, avoiding write amplification on the tickets branch, cryptographic binding without new storage primitives, and zero migration cost.
evidence: ABSENCE: no paragraph or sentence explaining why the signed-pin approach was chosen as the mechanism (as opposed to an alternative structural approach).

Individual rationale clauses exist: 'no new CLI operation', 'no migration or eager backfill', 'natural rather than eager propagation' — but these are properties, not a positive explanation of WHY this approach fits best.
'Propagation is deliberately natural rather than eager' gestures at the choice but does not explain why natural propagation is correct for this problem.
findings.finding: The plan does not state a positive rationale for the overall architectural choice of 'signed pins as separate attestation metadata with pull-based drift detection' — it describes individual decisions but never explains why this mechanism was preferred over structural alternatives (e.g. push-based invalidation events, or incorporating prerequisite fingerprints into the subject's material hash).
findings.location: Settled design (entire section) / Outcome
scenarios: Implementer reading the plan cannot distinguish whether the signed-pin mechanism was chosen for cryptographic binding strength, consistency with existing compute_validity philosophy, write-amplification avoidance, or simplicity — making it hard to evaluate tradeoffs when edge cases arise during implementation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document why the bespoke `plan-material-pin` encoding is preferred over an in-toto link-attestation payload shape already supported by the DSSE/op-cert substrate (ADR 0044/0049), or confirm the two are compatible and a conscious choice was made to not reuse the link model.
evidence: Plan: 'New plan reviews always emit one unique plan-material-pin per direct child or prerequisite, keyed by authoritative canonical ticket ID and normalized role child or prerequisite.'
ADR 0044 §Prior art: 'DSSE protocol v1.0.0 (secure-systems-lab/dsse); in-toto attestation; OpenSSH PROTOCOL.sshsig'
ADR 0049: 'reuses SLSA-style non-falsifiable provenance' and in-toto subject binding
ABSENCE: no section in the plan discusses in-toto link attestation as an alternative vehicle for the new pin data
findings.finding: The plan-material pins mechanism—pinning a related ticket's canonical ID + role + narrow material fingerprint inside a plan-review attestation—is structurally equivalent to what in-toto 'link' attestations do for supply-chain dependency provenance. ADR 0044 already imports in-toto/SLSA/DSSE as the attestation substrate (`attest/dsse.py`, `attest/opcert.py`). The plan does not discuss whether reusing in-toto link-attestation payloads (already present in the substrate) as the vehicle for dependency pins was evaluated, or why a new bespoke `plan-material-pin` field is preferred over an established in-toto link payload encoding.
findings.location: Settled design / Signed plan-material pins
scenarios: A later consumer attempts to verify pin data using the in-toto toolchain and finds the custom pin format is incompatible, requiring a bespoke verifier rather than leveraging the substrate already in place.
The new 'plan-material-pin' field encoding diverges from the op-cert DSSE payload shape (ADR 0049 §3) and requires separate attestation verification logic, increasing maintenance surface.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a post-rollback schema-state assertion: specify whether pin fields are forward-compatible/ignorable by older code versions, document the attestation validity state after rollback (e.g., pins are stripped or treated as absent), and confirm no ticket is left in an unrecoverable certification state after a code rollback with enforcement enabled.
evidence: 'Re-enabling enforcement immediately applies existing pin-aware attestations.' — but the inverse (rolling back code that understands pins) is unaddressed.
'The rebar project enables enforcement.' — means enforcement will be active in production, making rollback state load-bearing.
ABSENCE: no statement that pin fields are additive/ignorable by prior code versions, and no assertion of schema state post-rollback.
DATA LOSS RISK: if rolled-back code rejects pin-carrying attestations as malformed, previously-certified tickets lose their valid attestation, blocking claim/close operations.
findings.finding: The plan does not assert the schema/attestation state after a code rollback that removes pin support. Once pin-carrying attestations are persisted and enforcement is enabled on the rebar project, rolling back the feature code leaves the system in an unspecified state: older code may reject, misread, or silently pass pin-carrying attestations, and the plan provides no guidance on this post-rollback correctness guarantee.
findings.location: Settled design → Enforcement and rollout; Acceptance Criteria AC-6
scenarios: A deployment rolls out pin support and enables enforcement on rebar; a subsequent rollback to the prior code version encounters pin-carrying attestations and either rejects them (breaking claim/close) or silently ignores pins (defeating enforcement).
A partial rollout where some nodes run old code and some run new code simultaneously — old nodes cannot interpret pins and may make inconsistent validity decisions.
findings.suggested_fix: Declare pin metadata as an optional additive field in the attestation schema: older code that does not recognize it treats the attestation as pin-free (legacy-valid). Document this contract explicitly in the design. If this guarantee cannot be made, add a compensating-forward procedure (e.g., enforcement must be disabled before rollback, or a pin-stripping migration step is provided) and call out the DATA LOSS RISK for any window where enforcement is on and old code is active.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the `plan-material-pin` manifest line format (prefix constant, field order: role, ticket ID, fingerprint hash) co-located with the other manifest line definitions in `manifest.py` or the Settled design section.
evidence: Plan: 'one unique plan-material-pin per direct child or prerequisite, keyed by authoritative canonical ticket ID and normalized role child or prerequisite'
Plan: 'Pins reuse the existing material fingerprint format.'
Confirmed via read of manifest.py: no `pin` prefix constant, no pin-line builder, no pin parser exists — the plan introduces this as a manifest extension but never specifies the line syntax.
The existing `dep <sha256> <path>` line format shows a per-field positional pattern; pins need at minimum `pin <role> <ticket_id> <fingerprint>` but this is not stated.
findings.finding: The `plan-material-pin` serialization format is never defined. The plan states pins 'reuse the existing material fingerprint format' and are 'automatic signed attestation metadata', but the material fingerprint is a bare hash string, not a structured record. A pin must encode three fields (canonical ticket ID, normalized role, fingerprint hash), yet no manifest line prefix, field ordering, or encoding is specified anywhere in the plan.
findings.location: Settled design > Signed plan-material pins; manifest.py (confirmed absent)
scenarios: Implementers cannot write the manifest builder extension or the pin parser without inventing the format.
The enforcement consumer that checks pin drift must parse these lines — without a defined format it cannot be implemented or tested.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Define the exact string values for the normalized role enum ('child', 'prerequisite') and specify the conflict-resolution rule when a ticket qualifies as both.
evidence: Plan: 'keyed by authoritative canonical ticket ID and normalized role child or prerequisite'

Plan: 'Direct prerequisites are the union of outgoing depends_on targets and inbound blocks sources' — a ticket could simultaneously be a child (in the parent/child hierarchy) and a prerequisite (via depends_on/blocks), requiring a conflict-resolution rule.
No enum definition or conflict rule is present anywhere in the plan.
findings.finding: The 'normalized role' enum for plan-material-pins is incompletely specified. The plan says the role is 'child or prerequisite', but does not define: (1) the exact string values, (2) what role is assigned when a ticket is simultaneously a direct child AND a direct prerequisite, and (3) whether any other role values are possible (e.g., 'parent' for symmetry). This creates an ambiguous key space for the pin collection.
findings.location: Settled design > Signed plan-material pins
scenarios: A ticket that is both a child and a prerequisite could be pinned twice (once per role) or once (with an unspecified precedence rule), making the 'one unique pin per target' guarantee ambiguous.
A consumer checking pins cannot validate the role field without knowing the allowed vocabulary.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the interaction between all-or-nothing pin collection and the oversized-prerequisite too-large remediation path: specify whether an oversized prerequisite prevents signing (consistent with all-or-nothing) or whether a too-large finding is emitted and a pin is omitted for that target only.
evidence: Plan: 'Pin collection is all-or-nothing: unresolved, malformed, duplicate, or unreadable required targets prevent signing.'
Plan: 'Context-limit failures retry with smaller bins. Subject plus one oversized prerequisite yields the existing too-large remediation.'
These two rules are in separate sections with no cross-reference resolving their interaction.
findings.finding: The fallback for an oversized single-prerequisite pairing creates an unspecified contradiction with the all-or-nothing pin collection rule. The plan states 'Pin collection is all-or-nothing: unresolved, malformed, duplicate, or unreadable required targets prevent signing' AND 'Subject plus one oversized prerequisite yields the existing too-large remediation.' But if a prerequisite is too large to evaluate, it is effectively unresolvable — it is unclear whether this triggers the all-or-nothing signing abort or the too-large finding path, and the plan provides no decision rule to resolve the conflict.
findings.location: Settled design > Signed plan-material pins; Settled design > Prerequisite consistency review
scenarios: A large prerequisite ticket exceeds the context window: does signing abort entirely (all-or-nothing), or does a too-large finding get emitted and signing proceeds without a pin for that prerequisite?
An agent implementation must choose one behavior; a wrong choice either blocks all large epics from ever getting a signed review, or silently omits pin coverage for oversized prerequisites.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
